role_summary: Co-founder (with husband Millard Fuller) of Habitat for Humanity (1976) and the Fuller Center for Housing (2005), the Christian-economic-justice housing-construction movements that grew out of the Koinonia Farm intentional community in Americus, Georgia.
ai_reading: Fuller Degelmann's top semantic neighbors are Shirley Miller Sherrod (entry 117, count 96, USDA Black-farmer-advocacy figure), H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, count 87, Mississippi community-health-center founder), James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Gertrude Newsome Jackson (entry 136), and Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC organizer). The cluster reads her as part of a Christian-economic-justice and rural-Southern-organizing cohort that intersects medical, agricultural, and housing organizing, not strictly a civil rights movement cluster. Her testimony's vector signature places her near the figures whose movement work crossed into community-economic-development; the Koinonia Farm and Habitat for Humanity origins are Southern rural Christian projects whose embedding signature the catalog reads as overlapping with rural-medicine (Geiger), Black-farming-policy (Sherrod), and Mississippi voter-registration (McLaurin) more than with the Northern liberal Christian organizing the cultural reception of Habitat for Humanity often emphasizes.
biographical_paragraph: Linda Fuller Degelmann co-founded Habitat for Humanity with her husband Millard Fuller in 1976 and the Fuller Center for Housing in 2005, after both organizations grew out of the Koinonia Farm intentional community near Americus, Georgia [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing under Wikipedia and the Fuller Center website returned 404 for the slug variants searched in this iteration; the strongest available signal for her movement role is the embedding's cosine with the rural-Southern-Christian-economic-justice cohort visible in her semantic neighbors. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 78 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Linda Fuller Degelmann
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fuller Degelmann describes how the partnership-housing model began at Koinonia Farm, the project that later became Habitat for Humanity, after she and Millard Fuller saw the housing of Black sharecropping families in Sumter County, Georgia:
interview_snippets.quote: But we had some property at Koinonia Farm and we said, well why don't we start what we call a partnership housing movement. We're in partnership with the people that need the help. We're in partnership with God who has given us the direction to help our brothers and sisters in need. Since Blacks could not go to the bank to get loans for to buy house or even by land, we thought well we'll just dedicate some of the land here, the 2000 something acres at Koinonia Farm, and start building houses. Sell them to the families, no profit, no interest, that was the only way the families could afford it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Linda Fuller Degelmann
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the condition of the sharecropper houses she and Millard Fuller visited around Koinonia Farm before they began building:
interview_snippets.quote: We would visit some of these Shacks and there were big holes in the roofs, holes in the floors. Newspapers had been tacked up on the walls to insulate as best as they could.

interview_snippets.speaker: Linda Fuller Degelmann
interview_snippets.lead_in: A police chief in Unadilla, Georgia arrested Millard Fuller for visiting a Black family that was integrating the local white high school. Fuller Degelmann, holding her infant daughter, responded:
interview_snippets.quote: And so I walked down and I said, if my husband was doing something wrong, I was too. You got to arrest me too.

interview_snippets.speaker: Linda Fuller Degelmann
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling the night an interracial group from Koinonia Farm attended a Christmas service at the segregated First Baptist Church in Americus, Georgia, where the congregation moved away from their pew:
interview_snippets.quote: Same thing happened to the people that were seated behind us. They got up and moved somewhere else. So here we were like an island on this one pew in the church.

interview_snippets.speaker: William S. Leventhal
interview_snippets.lead_in: William S. Leventhal, a journalist who covered the Alabama movement, recalls meeting the Fullers and writing an early article on their housing work:
interview_snippets.quote: Millard Fuller from, from Lanett, Alabama and his wife, Linda Fuller from Montgomery, who started Habitat, who I met, I wrote the first article about Millard in housing, because that started at Koinonia Farm

movement_context: Koinonia Farm was established in 1942 in Sumter County, Georgia, by two couples: Clarence and Florence Jordan together with Martin and Mabel England [src: 2]. Clarence Jordan, a Southern Baptist with a doctorate in Greek New Testament from Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, framed the settlement as a working demonstration of the Acts 2 community of shared resources and racial integration in the heart of rural southwest Georgia [src: 3]. Through the 1950s, Koinonia's interracial worship and pay practices drew sustained violent reprisal: Ku Klux Klan night riders shot into farm buildings, dynamite damaged the roadside market, and a local boycott shut the community out of seed, feed, and credit through Sumter County merchants [src: 2]. Koinonia answered the boycott by mail-ordering pecans and other farm products to a national support network, which became the community's economic spine and remained so after the worst of the violence subsided [src: 2]. Millard and Linda Fuller arrived at Koinonia in 1968 as a young couple who had walked away from a self-made marketing fortune in Alabama; they apprenticed under Clarence Jordan in the year before his sudden death in 1969 [src: 4]. With Jordan and others, the Fullers developed the Partnership Housing concept: simple homes built alongside the future residents, sold at construction cost, financed through no-interest loans drawn from a revolving Fund for Humanity into which homeowner payments and outside donations were returned [src: 5]. Millard cited Exodus 22:25 as the scriptural anchor for the no-interest rule and described the model as the Economics of Jesus [src: 4]. The Fullers tested the partnership-housing approach in Mbandaka, Zaire (now Democratic Republic of the Congo) from 1973 to 1976 [src: 4][src: 5], and on their return formally incorporated Habitat for Humanity International in 1976, with headquarters established in Americus next door to the Koinonia property [src: 5]. Former U.S. President Jimmy Carter and his wife Rosalynn joined the work as volunteer carpenters; their annual Carter Work Project build week, first held in 1984, expanded Habitat's public profile and contributed to its growth across all fifty U.S. states and more than seventy other countries [src: 5][src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: By 2005 Habitat for Humanity International was operating in more than seventy countries and had built homes alongside hundreds of thousands of partner families since 1976 [src: 5]. In early 2005 the Habitat board terminated Millard Fuller's role at the organization after a period of internal conflict, and Linda Fuller departed with him [src: 4][src: 6]. The two responded by founding the Fuller Center for Housing in April 2005, holding the inaugural board meeting at Koinonia Farm and framing the new ministry as a return to the partnership-housing roots of the original 1976 project [src: 6][src: 2]. Millard Fuller died on February 3, 2009, at the age of seventy-four, and was buried at Koinonia Farm [src: 4]. Linda Fuller continued in the leadership of the Fuller Center after his death, remained a recognized presence on Fuller Center build sites, and received honorary doctorates and the Harry S. Truman Public Service Award (1994) for her work in affordable housing [src: 6]. She remarried Paul Degelmann in June 2011; Degelmann died in March 2021 [src: 6]. The Civil Rights History Project recorded her oral history as entry 78 of AFC 2010/039, the Smithsonian-Library of Congress documentation collection, where her testimony sits in the rural-Southern-Christian-economic-justice cluster that the embedding space surfaces around her [src: 1].
